text: Temporal distortion is one of the most consistent markers of deep flow states. Neuroscientists studying peak performance found that the brain's insular cortex — responsible for interoception and tracking the passage of time — reduces its activity during intense absorption. This is not mere distraction; it is a coordinated suppression. The self-referential default mode network also quiets, eliminating the background narration of 'how am I doing?' and 'how long has it been?' The result is a compression of experienced duration: hours collapse into what feels like minutes. Athletes, surgeons, and programmers all report this same dissolution of clock-time during their most productive episodes.
question: Which brain region does the passage identify as responsible for tracking the passage of time?
